title: Creating a wildlife pond for surface water retention
subtitle: Siting, excavation, liner choice, planting zones, and how the pond reduces runoff from the garden
excerpt: A garden pond serves dual functions: it slows and holds surface water from hard surfaces and roof drainage, and it supports wildlife. A well-designed 6 to 10 m² pond can hold 3,000 to 6,000 litres of storm water temporarily, reducing peak runoff from the garden by a significant amount. This guide covers siting, excavation, liner selection, and the planting zones that make the pond self-sustaining.
sourceNotes: Royal Horticultural Society guidance on garden pond construction. Wildlife Trusts guidance on wildlife pond design. CIRIA SuDS manual on pond design for sustainable drainage. Environment Agency guidance on planting of native pond plants. Natural England guidance on creating wildlife habitat ponds.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flexible membrane used to waterproof a pond excavation. EPDM (ethylene propylene diene monomer) liners are the most durable for wildlife ponds, with a lifespan of 30 to 50 years. Butyl rubber is similar. Rigid pre-formed liners are shorter-lived and harder to repair.

The shallow-water zone at the pond edge, typically 5 to 20 cm deep, where marginal aquatic plants (yellow flag iris, marsh marigold, water mint) are planted. Marginals provide habitat, slow water entry into the pond, and filter sediment.

content.text: A 7 m² pond at 600 mm maximum depth holds around 4,200 litres of water. As storm water enters, the pond rises to overflow level, at which point it discharges slowly through a controlled outlet to a border or a soakaway. The 4,200 litres of buffer storage holds back the peak runoff from a 50 to 100 m² hard surface area during a moderate storm.
Minimum 3 m from any building, 1.5 m from the property boundary.
At least 50 percent of the surface in sun for at least 6 hours on a summer day. North-facing ponds in full shade develop algae and few invertebrates.
Not directly under deciduous trees. Fallen leaves acidify and deoxygenate the water as they decompose.
Where surface water naturally collects: a low corner, the base of a slope, or at the end of a downpipe outlet area.
Mark the outline with sand or a hosepipe. Create shelved edges: a 300 mm wide x 100 mm deep shelf around 30 percent of the perimeter for the
, then a slope down to the maximum depth (minimum 600 mm to prevent freezing in hard winters).
Remove all sharp stones and roots from the excavation. Line the base with 50 mm of damp sand.

Liner sizing formula: add twice the maximum depth to both length and width measurements, then add 500 mm each way for anchoring. A 3 m x 2 m pond at 600 mm depth needs (3 + 1.2 + 0.5) x (2 + 1.2 + 0.5) = 4.7 x 3.7 m of liner.
. On a warm day the liner is more flexible and easier to fold at the corners. Fill with water before trimming the edges; the weight will pull the liner into the contours.
Trim the edge and anchor with stone, turf, or a proprietary edging rail.

Fill with rainwater from a water butt, or leave empty for rain to fill naturally over several weeks. Do not use mains water: the chlorine and high mineral content inhibit aquatic life. Plant native marginals in the shallow shelf in mesh baskets with aquatic compost and gravel topdressing. Oxygenating plants (native waterweed like hornwort or ceratophyllum) go on the deep floor in baskets.

attrs.symptom: Pond water turns bright green within 2 weeks

content.text: Algal bloom from excess nutrients and high light. Plant more oxygenating plants to compete for nutrients. Add a floating-leaf plant (native water lily) to shade 40 percent of the surface. Avoid adding pond feed or tap water. The bloom usually subsides within 6 to 8 weeks as the ecosystem establishes.
